category_title: Oppositional and boisterous students with disruptive behaviors
minimal_context: Edward is standing behind Trey at the computer during morning menu to collaborate on a project.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Edward, it is time for morning menu. Please work on the computer project with Trey.

STUDENT: (Standing behind Trey) The Astronomy Picture of the Day. Saturn’s moon has (inaudible) on it. Whoops! Comet over California. Whoosh!

TUTOR: Remember to share the computer.

STUDENT: Alien monster gods. Go back there, Trey. I found something that’s like radar, and it’s so cool . . .

TUTOR: You need to compromise with your partner.

STUDENT: (Loudly) No! I'm not compromising! Trey is doing it wrong! Move!

options.A: Adjust the computer programs and applications to his personal learning characteristics during the morning menu period.
options.B: Check his progress on the computer platform weekly to see what mistakes he is making and what topics he is struggling with.
options.C: Use large fonts with supporting pictures on the computer, reduce project questions, and provide word banks and speech-generating devices.
options.D: Persuade peers to allow him to join their computer projects and keep a close eye on the group dynamics.
